mnemonics: PHAT P-Values: P-Hat Assesses Truth. P-value < α? Then Ho is Probably History!
N.O.T. (Null, Observed, Test): Always state your Null hypothesis, calculate your Observed statistic, then choose your Test.
α-B-C: Alpha Before Conclusion. Set your significance level BEFORE you run the test and see the data.
keyPoints: A test of significance assesses the evidence provided by data against a null hypothesis (H₀) and in favor of an alternative hypothesis (Hₐ).
The p-value is the probability, assuming the null hypothesis is true, of obtaining a test statistic at least as extreme as the one actually observed.

A result is statistically significant if the observed effect is unlikely to be due to chance alone, typically when the p-value is less than a pre-specified significance level (α), such as 0.05 or 0.01.
commonMistakes: Concluding that a statistically significant result proves the alternative hypothesis is true. Significance tests only provide evidence against the null hypothesis.

Confusing statistical significance with practical significance. A result can be statistically significant (unlikely by chance) but have such a small effect size that it is meaningless in a real-world context.
Using a one-sided test (e.g., μ > 0) when a two-sided test (e.g., μ ≠ 0) is appropriate, often because the one-sided test has a lower threshold for significance, making it easier to reject H₀.
